summary: I was born in Hiroshima, Japan, on November 20, 1870, during the 20th year of Meiji. My father was a farmer there, and we lived in a village in Jinseki District, Nagato. Hiroshima was my home before I left for America as a young man.

I was born in the 20th year of Meiji, which corresponds to 1870. The Meiji era was a time of great change in Japan, and during my childhood, events like the Sino-Japanese War and Russo-Japanese War took place. My schooling and early life were shaped by the customs and education of that era.

I remember hearing about Ninomiya Sontoku during my childhood, though we only had a few books at home. He was a figure known to us, likely because of his teachings and influence on farming and ethics. Though I can't recall much detail, his name was familiar in my early education.

My family practiced Buddhism when I was growing up in Japan. We belonged to the Buddhist faith, which was an important part of our daily lives and community. It shaped many of our values and traditions in the village.
Although we were Buddhists, we did not belong to the Shinshu sect. Instead, our family was part of the Zenshu sect. So, Shinshu was not the branch of Buddhism that influenced my upbringing.

Our family belonged to the Zenshu sect of Buddhism while I was growing up in Jinseki District, Nagato village. This sect was part of our religious life and community identity in Japan. It provided the spiritual framework during my childhood.

I grew up in a village called Nagato in Jinseki District. It was a rural area where my father farmed and where our community was close-knit. Life there was simple, and I attended a local school with a teacher named Mr. Tange Gunpei.

Nagato village in Hiroshima Prefecture was my childhood home. It was a small farming village in Jinseki District where my family lived and worked. I remember the village life and the times we saw men leave for the Sino-Japanese War.

My first school teacher was Mr. Tange Gunpei, a very fine man who rarely got angry. He taught us mostly reading and morals during my early education in Japan. I remember he would only reprimand us if we were late or misbehaved on the way to school.

During my school years in Japan, the Sino-Japanese War was ongoing around the 7th and 8th year of Meiji. I recall going to the village to see off the men who were sent to fight. One man from our village even returned with a medal of honor, though he sadly lost it due to an unfortunate incident.

The Russo-Japanese War ended just as I arrived in America around 1906. I remember the village farewells for the men going off to fight in that war. It was a significant event marking the transition from my life in Japan to my new life abroad.

I came to the United States in 1906 after spending a year in Hawaii. My journey began in Japan, and after arriving in Oakland because San Francisco was destroyed by fire, I worked various jobs including railroads, sugar beet fields, and restaurants. The U.S. was a land of opportunity and adventure for me as a young man.

Before coming to the mainland United States, I spent about one year in Hawaii working in the sugar cane fields. The work was very hard, paying $1.65 a day with deductions for board and room. It was my first experience abroad and a stepping stone before moving to the U.S. mainland.

When I first arrived in America in 1906, we could not land in San Francisco because the city had been destroyed by fire. Instead, we landed in Oakland, California. This unexpected change shaped the beginning of my life in the United States.

After arriving in Oakland in 1906 due to the San Francisco fire, I immediately sought employment. I worked on the railroad and slept in a boxcar while waiting for my assignment. Oakland was my first foothold in America.

I traveled to America aboard the ship Manchuria, which was a large vessel with mostly Chinese crew and passengers. The journey was my first experience crossing the ocean to a new continent. I was about 17 years old and full of enthusiasm for adventure.

While working on the railroad after arriving in Oakland, I was assigned to a section that took me to Cheyenne, Wyoming. We stayed there a few days before I was moved to another railroad section. It was part of my early work experience in the U.S.

After working on the railroad, I heard there was a need for labor in the sugar beet fields in Colorado. I tried working there but found the work too strenuous and quit after a month. Later, I moved to Denver and worked in a Japanese-owned boarding house.

I lived in Denver for about two years, working in a Japanese-owned boarding house where I made beds and did other chores. There were only a few Japanese people in Denver then, including a restaurant and a barber shop. It was a modest but important part of my early life in America.

In 1908, I moved to Seattle and stayed there about two years. I worked on ships as a cabin steward, which was interesting because the cook was Japanese and I could talk to him. Seattle was also where I worked in a bar and enjoyed leisure activities like playing pool.

While working on a ship out of Seattle, we first headed to New Mexico, though we were not loaded with cargo there. It was part of my early maritime work experience before traveling to Australia and back to the U.S.

On a ship trip from Seattle, we stopped in Newcastle, New South Wales, Australia, where our schooner was loaded with coal. I was impressed by the beauty of Australia, especially the big chestnut trees. Though I lived on the ship, I wanted to return to America rather than settle there.

After loading coal in Newcastle, Australia, the ship returned to Portland, Oregon. I came back to Seattle after that trip. Portland was part of my early travels while working on ships along the Pacific coast.

I visited Australia while working on a ship, stopping in Newcastle, New South Wales. I found Australia to be a very lovely place with beautiful chestnut trees. Despite its charm, I chose to return to America rather than settle there.

In Tacoma, Washington, I met a few people who came from the same district in Japan as I did. It was comforting to find familiar faces so far from home. Tacoma was one of the places I lived and worked during my early years in America.

I had a strong desire to go to Alaska, but you needed at least $50 to get there. Unfortunately, I saved the money but it was stolen by a fellow Japanese worker. This was a painful experience that kept me from fulfilling that dream.

After leaving Seattle, I moved to Sacramento, California, hoping to work as a barber. Unfortunately, I was not successful in that trade. Sacramento was a brief stop before I moved on to Stockton to work in the potato fields.

In Stockton, I worked in the potato fields during the winter and later bought a pool room with my savings. Eventually, I sold the pool room and bought a restaurant, which was a big responsibility since I had to hire cooks and helpers. Stockton was where I settled down and started a family.

When my bride came from Japan in 1911, I went to Angel Island in San Francisco to meet her. She had to show $50 to be allowed to go ashore, and I borrowed money to help her through the immigration process. Angel Island was the gateway for many Japanese immigrants like us.
